title: Generic method
instruction:
A generic method uses `<T>` as a placeholder type.

Run this.
successMessage: Max works for any type that implements IComparable<T>. The where clause constrains T.

title: Generic class
instruction:
A generic class holds data of any type while remaining type-safe.

Run this.
successMessage: Box<int> and Box<string> are two separate types generated from the same generic class. No casting needed.

title: Generic Stack
instruction:
Implement a stack that works for any element type.

Run this.
successMessage: MyStack<T> works for strings, ints, or any other type. [^1] is a C# 8 index-from-end — the last element.

title: Constraints (where)
instruction:
`where T : ...` constrains what types can be used.

Run this.
successMessage: where T : new() requires T to have a parameterless constructor. Other constraints: class (reference type), struct (value type), IComparable<T>, specific base class.

title: Multiple type parameters
instruction:
Generics can have multiple type parameters.

Run this.
successMessage: Multiple type parameters map to completely independent types. Pair<string,int> and Pair<int,bool> are different classes.

title: default(T)
instruction:
`default(T)` returns the default value for any type (null for reference types, 0 for numerics).

Run this.
successMessage: default(T) is 0 for int, false for bool, null for reference types. Works for any T.

title: Generic repository pattern
instruction:
Generics are the foundation of most framework patterns, like the repository.

Run this.
successMessage: Repository<T> is completely generic — reuse it for Product, User, Order, anything. This is a simplified version of a very common enterprise pattern.

title: Covariance with out
instruction:
`IEnumerable<out T>` allows assigning a List<Dog> to IEnumerable<Animal>.

Run this.
successMessage: IEnumerable<out T> is covariant — a sequence of Dogs IS a sequence of Animals. This only works for interfaces declared with 'out'.
